input_row.title: Ensure SSH Idle Timeout Interval is configured
input_row.description: NOTE: To clarify, the two settings described below is only meant for idle connections from a protocol perspective and not meant to check if the user is active or not. An idle user does not mean an idle connection. SSH does not and never had, intentionally, the capability to drop idle users. In SSH versions before 8.2p1 there was a bug that caused these values to behave in such a manner that they where abused to disconnect idle users. This bug has been resolved in 8.2p1 and thus it can no longer be abused disconnect idle users. The two options ClientAliveInterval and ClientAliveCountMax control the timeout of SSH sessions. Taken directly from man 5 sshd_config: • ClientAliveInterval Sets a timeout interval in seconds after which if no data has been received from the client, sshd(8) will send a message through the encrypted channel to request a response from the client. The default is 0, indicating that these messages will not be sent to the client. • ClientAliveCountMax Sets the number of client alive messages which may be sent without sshd(8) receiving any messages back from the client. If this threshold is reached while client alive messages are being sent, sshd will disconnect the client, terminating the session. It is important to note that the use of client alive messages is very different from TCPKeepAlive. The client alive messages are sent through the encrypted channel and therefore will not be spoofable. The TCP keepalive option en‐abled by TCPKeepAlive is spoofable. The client alive mechanism is valuable when the client or server depend on knowing when a connection has become unresponsive. The default value is 3. If ClientAliveInterval is set to 15, and ClientAliveCountMax is left at the default, unresponsive SSH clients will be disconnected after approximately 45 seconds. Setting a zero ClientAliveCountMax disables connection termination.
input_row.rationale: In order to prevent resource exhaustion, appropriate values should be set for both ClientAliveInterval and ClientAliveCountMax. Specifically, looking at the source code, ClientAliveCountMax must be greater than zero in order to utilize the ability of SSH to drop idle connections. If connections are allowed to stay open indefinately, this can potentially be used as a DDOS attack or simple resource exhaustion could occur over unreliable networks. The example set here is a 45 second timeout. Consult your site policy for network timeouts and apply as appropriate.
input_row.audit: Run the following commands and verify ClientAliveInterval is greater than zero: # sshd -T -C user=root -C host="$(hostname)" -C addr="$(grep $(hostname) /etc/hosts | awk '{print $1}')" | grep clientaliveinterval Example output: clientaliveinterval 15 Run the following command and verify ClientAliveCountMax is greater than zero: # sshd -T -C user=root -C host="$(hostname)" -C addr="$(grep $(hostname) /etc/hosts | awk '{print $1}')" | grep clientalivecountmax Example output: clientalivecountmax 3 Run the following command: # grep -Pis '^\h*ClientAliveCountMax\h+"?0\b' /etc/ssh/sshd_config /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf Nothing should be returned Note: If Include locations besides, or in addition to /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf and/or Match set statements are used in your environment, those locations should be checked for the correct configuration as well.
input_row.remediation: Edit the /etc/ssh/sshd_config file to set the parameters above any Include entries according to site policy. Example: ClientAliveInterval 15 ClientAliveCountMax 3 Note: First occurrence of a option takes precedence, Match set statements withstanding. If Include locations are enabled, used, and order of precedence is understood in your environment, the entry may be created in a file in Include location. Default Value: ClientAliveInterval 0 ClientAliveCountMax 3 References: 1. https://man.openbsd.org/sshd_config 2. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CM-1, CM-2, CM-6, CM-7, IA-5 Additional Information: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1873547 https://github.com/openssh/openssh-portable/blob/V_8_9/serverloop.c#L137 MITRE ATT&CK Mappings: Techniques / Sub- techniques Tactics Mitigations T1078, T1078.001, T1078.002, T1078.003 TA0001 M1026 5.3 Configure privilege escalation There are various tools which allows a permitted user to execute a command as the superuser or another user, as specified by the security policy. sudo https://www.sudo.ws/ The invoking user's real (not effective) user ID is used to determine the user name with which to query the security policy. sudo supports a plug-in architecture for security policies and input/output logging. Third parties can develop and distribute their own policy and I/O logging plug-ins to work seamlessly with the sudo front end. The default security policy is sudoers, which is configured via the file /etc/sudoers and any entries in /etc/sudoers.d. pkexec https://www.freedesktop.org/software/polkit/docs/0.105/pkexec.1.html
input_row.reference: 1. https://man.openbsd.org/sshd_config 2. NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CM-1, CM-2, CM-6, CM-7, IA-5 Additional Information: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1873547 https://github.com/openssh/openssh-portable/blob/V_8_9/serverloop.c#L137 MITRE ATT&CK Mappings: Techniques / Sub- techniques Tactics Mitigations T1078, T1078.001, T1078.002, T1078.003 TA0001 M1026 5.3 Configure privilege escalation There are various tools which allows a permitted user to execute a command as the superuser or another user, as specified by the security policy. sudo https://www.sudo.ws/ The invoking user's real (not effective) user ID is used to determine the user name with which to query the security policy. sudo supports a plug-in architecture for security policies and input/output logging. Third parties can develop and distribute their own policy and I/O logging plug-ins to work seamlessly with the sudo front end. The default security policy is sudoers, which is configured via the file /etc/sudoers and any entries in /etc/sudoers.d. pkexec https://www.freedesktop.org/software/polkit/docs/0.105/pkexec.1.html

first_opinion.response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Access the Azure portal**:
   - Navigate to the Azure portal (https://portal.azure.com).
   - Sign in with appropriate privileges to access the Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) cluster.

2. **SSH to the AKS nodes**:
   - Use SSH command to access one of the nodes in the AKS cluster. You would typically use:
     ```
     ssh azureuser@<node-ip-address>
     ```
   - Replace `<node-ip-address>` with the actual IP address of a node in your AKS cluster.
   - You may need to obtain the node IP addresses from the Azure portal within your AKS resource details.

3. **Verify SSH configuration**:
   - Once logged into the node, run the following command to check the `ClientAliveInterval` configuration:
     ```
     sshd -T | grep clientaliveinterval
     ```
   - Next, check the `ClientAliveCountMax` configuration with:
     ```
     sshd -T | grep clientalivecountmax
     ```

4. **Check the configuration file**:
   - Examine the `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` file or any included files for the respective parameters by executing:
     ```
     grep -Pis '^\\h*ClientAliveCountMax\\h+\"?0\\b' /etc/ssh/sshd_config /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf
     ```
   - Ensure that the output is empty, which indicates `ClientAliveCountMax` is not set to zero.

5. **Expected secure configuration**:
   - Verify that `ClientAliveInterval` is set to a value greater than 0 (recommended: 15) and that `ClientAliveCountMax` is set to a value greater than 0 (recommended: 3).

6. **Confirm compliance**:
   - If both `ClientAliveInterval` and `ClientAliveCountMax` meet the recommended values and the configuration file does not return anything for zero settings, the check is compliant.
   - Document the findings and configurations for compliance reporting.

second_opinion.response:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:** The current classification as "Manual" is partially correct. While the manual steps provided are thorough for a direct SSH configuration check, there are automated ways to assess this control using Azure capabilities.
- **Azure APIs/CLI Capabilities:** Azure Monitor and Azure Policy could be leveraged to automate the compliance check for SSH configurations. Specifically, using Azure Policy to audit configurations and Azure Monitor to track logs for SSH sessions could automate parts of this process. Additionally, Azure CLI or Azure PowerShell can be scripted to check SSH configurations across nodes, reducing the need for manual SSH access.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 7

- **Automation Details:** The manual steps are technically accurate for checking SSH configurations directly on AKS nodes. However, they lack integration with Azure's native tools for automation.
- **Manual Steps:** The steps are mostly accurate and complete for a manual approach, but they assume direct SSH access, which may not always be feasible or secure in production environments.
- **Program Naming Convention:** The naming convention for the program is not applicable here as the steps are manual and not encapsulated in a script or tool.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Automation Suggestions:** Implement Azure Policy to audit SSH configurations across AKS nodes. Use Azure CLI or PowerShell scripts to automate the retrieval and checking of SSH settings.
- **Security Considerations:** Direct SSH access to AKS nodes should be minimized. Consider using Azure Bastion or Just-in-Time VM access for secure and controlled SSH access.
- **Alternative Approach:** Utilize Azure Security Center to monitor and alert on SSH configuration deviations. This could provide a more centralized and automated compliance check.

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- While the manual steps are technically sound, the lack of integration with Azure's automation capabilities reduces confidence in the approach's efficiency and scalability.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- **Explanation:** Re-classify the approach to include automated checks using Azure Policy and Azure Monitor. Develop scripts using Azure CLI or PowerShell to automate SSH configuration checks across AKS nodes. This will enhance the efficiency, scalability, and security of the compliance assessment process.

final_decision.response:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
The compliance control for ensuring SSH Idle Timeout Interval can be automated using Azure's native tools. Azure Policy can be configured to audit SSH configurations across AKS nodes, and Azure Monitor can track logs for SSH sessions, eliminating the need for manual SSH access. This approach leverages Azure's capabilities to automate compliance checks efficiently.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
azure_aks_cluster_ssh_idle_timeout_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Azure Resource/Service: AKS Clusters
- API/CLI Method: Azure Policy and Azure Monitor
- Properties to Check: SSH configuration settings for `ClientAliveInterval` and `ClientAliveCountMax`
- Pass Criteria: `ClientAliveInterval` > 0 and `ClientAliveCountMax` > 0
- Fail Criteria: `ClientAliveInterval` <= 0 or `ClientAliveCountMax` <= 0

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
The first AI opinion suggested a manual approach, while the second AI opinion highlighted the potential for automation using Azure's capabilities. By considering Azure's robust policy and monitoring tools, the decision was made to automate the compliance check, resolving the disagreement in favor of leveraging Azure's automation capabilities.

**SOURCE:** CIS_AKS_OPTIMIZED_AZURE_LINUX_BENCHMARK_V1.0.0_updated_20250825_190750.json
